title: skopeo sync aborts if any of the image tags has issues
body:
We use skopeo to mirror an external registry into our internal registry. The process of mirroring involves our tool deciding which image to `sync`. A particular image has a large number of tags in the external registry from which we are syncing. For whatever reason, it appears that at least one such published tag of the image is corrupt, because when we run the following `sync` command, it results into an error:

```
sync -s docker -d docker --dest-no-creds --dest-tls-verify=false --src-tls-verify=false --src
-creds=user:pass foobarserver:5000/helloworld 192.168.1.2:5000
```
The error is as follows:

```
INFO[0010] Tag presence check                            imagename="foobarserver:5000/helloworld" tagged=false
INFO[0010] Getting tags                                  image="foobarserver:5000/helloworld"
INFO[0010] Copying image ref 1/157                       from="docker://foobarserver:5000/helloworld:release-i-v0.9.13" to="docker://192.168.1.2:5000/helloworld:release-i-v0.9.13"
Getting image source signatures
Skipping: image already present at destination
INFO[0010] Copying image ref 2/157                       from="docker://foobarserver:5000/helloworld:release_test_tag_i" to="docker://192.168.1.2:5000/helloworld:release_test_tag_i"
Getting image source signatures
Copying blob 1479d35649b2 skipped: already exists  
Copying blob b84b226fd20e skipped: already exists  
Copying blob 17dea04ab044 skipped: already exists  
Copying blob 1fd3936b9903 skipped: already exists  
Copying blob 67dc9985f582 skipped: already exists  
Copying blob 591b1660a002 skipped: already exists  
Copying blob c36ff2885a3f skipped: already exists  
Copying blob 3ac493f8fd0e [======================================] 403.5MiB / 403.7MiB
FATA[0017] Error copying ref "docker://foobarserver:5000/helloworld:release_test_tag_i": writing blob: Patch http://192.168.1.2:5000/v2/helloworld/blobs/uploads/af9456ed-ddfd-430a-b7be-4d017fa53b9f?_state=wfblgKXro5sof6DRRoeqE6oyDKQkLCy3YDfTsQzKqJx7Ik5hbWUiOiJteXNxbCIsIlVVSUQiOiJhZjk0NTZlZC1kZGZkLTQzMGEtYjdiZS00ZDAxN2ZhNTNiOWYiLCJPZmZzZXQiOjAsIlN0YXJ0ZWRBdCI6IjIwMjEtMDktMzBUMTM6NTQ6MDcuNzU3NDA3NjEzWiJ9: readfrom tcp 192.168.1.2:12508->192.168.1.2:5000: happened during read: Digest did not match, expected sha256:3ac493f8fd0e1d704f59ed568787ca99b0c60d56f1d0c28720881f56473e1a99, got sha256:ed56c64b031787de968682d738e2640cdc7aa03909c713cee8a7a1811d9e6d7a 
```
Do notice that it did manage to sync at least one tag but then failed for the `release_test_tag_i`. When it fails, it just aborts the sync process for this image. There's no way we can get past this since the tool doesn't have control over the external registry.

What would be ideal for us is if the `sync` process just warned about this issue and then moved ahead with the rest of the tags instead of aborting. If not by default then maybe by providing a command option/switch to introduce such a behaviour. Is this something that can be enhanced in skopeo?

comments.body:
> What would be ideal for us is if the `sync` process just warned about this issue and then moved ahead with the rest of the tags instead of aborting. If not by default then maybe by providing a command option/switch to introduce such a behaviour. Is this something that can be enhanced in skopeo?

Looking at the implementation of sync command, it looks like there are already places in this code flow where similar exceptions are logged and moved on instead of throwing an error. Like here https://github.com/containers/skopeo/blob/main/cmd/skopeo/sync.go#L341. So perhaps, we could do the same thing when image copy fails for a particular tag here https://github.com/containers/skopeo/blob/main/cmd/skopeo/sync.go#L614?
I would prefer --ignore, but basically allow it to return all images successfully and then fail in the end.
Thank you @rhatdan  and @vrothberg for the inputs. I've created a PR https://github.com/containers/skopeo/pull/1468 with the proposed changes.

FWIW ignoring failures seems to be a fairly indirect, and more importantly, imprecise/unreliable, way to for dealing with this situation. Consider what happens if some _other_ image that is actually important to you fails; will anyone notice?

- (Is the image truly corrupt, or is there some other bug that should be fixed?)
- Ideally, the image should just be fixed (if it is corrupt on disk, re-uploaded from a known-good copy with the same digest).
- If it is truly corrupt and unusable, and there is no other working copy available, it seems reasonable to me to just untag it (or perhaps tag it in a separate `foobarserver:5000/helloworld-broken-archive` repository, then untag it from the primary one)
- As far as `skopeo sync` is concerned, wouldn’t it make sense to express the users’ intent exactly, i.e. “mirror everything but $those_tags”? I’m afraid there isn’t a _convenient_ way to express exactly that right now; but the YAML format does support `images-by-tag-regex` which is, technically speaking, capable of expressing that (though in a very inconvenient way, to be sure).

I.e. should we really be adding an “ignore-failures” option, or an “exclude these tags” option?

Hello @mtrmac,

>     (Is the image truly corrupt, or is there some other bug that should be fixed?)
> 
>      Ideally, the image should just be fixed (if it is corrupt on disk, re-uploaded from a known-good copy with the same digest).
> 
>      If it is truly corrupt and unusable, and there is no other working copy available, it seems reasonable to me to just untag it (or perhaps tag it in a separate `foobarserver:5000/helloworld-broken-archive` repository, then untag it from the primary one)
> 
When I ran into this issue on our setup, I went back and checked the image (and a few other images which fail with same issue). All these images are genuinely corrupt and unusable (`docker pull` for those images fails with the same digest errors). So it isn't a bug in `skopeo`.

As for fixing the original images themselves, it's not straightforward at least in our case for multiple reasons. One, that registry from which we are mirroring gets pushed to by various team members almost every day. So it's likely that at some point someone will push such corrupt images again. Second, in my limited research of this area, I haven't found a way to untag or delete a tag for an image and whatever I have read so far in docker related forums/github repos is that they don't (and won't) support deleting already published tags. I will spend some more time to see if there are ways to do that, this coming week.


> As far as skopeo sync is concerned, wouldn’t it make sense to express the users’ intent exactly, i.e. “mirror everything but $those_tags”? I’m afraid there isn’t a convenient way to express exactly that right now; but the YAML format does support images-by-tag-regex which is, technically speaking, capable of expressing that (though in a very inconvenient way, to be sure).

This comes very close to the current use case we have which I noted in a different discussion here https://github.com/containers/skopeo/issues/1465#issuecomment-930771954. Specifically,

> We currently have a docker registry that is internal to our company and docker images get published to it very frequently. We then have a separate docker registry where we want to replicate certain images and only "recent X tags" for those images. Imagine something like "Keep last 10 builds" that's present in the Jenkins CI jobs. 

The regex based approach that you note, doesn't solve this for us (our tag names don't have a particular pattern). Nor does the current approach we are using to "sync all" tags, but at least the sync all approach allows us to have a superset of tags that we need. I still have to familiarize myself with Go and the skopeo codebase, but I was thinking that perhaps the `getImageTags` code in `sync.go` code is where it probably would be possible to provide hooks for "tag filters" or "tag selectors". The command could then perhaps allow "latest X" or "exact set of named tags" options? Like I said, I don't have much knowledge of skopeo code so not sure if this is feasible.

> So it's likely that at some point someone will push such corrupt images again.

(I’d be worried about my hardware reliability if that were a regular occurrence, but it’s not my system.)

---

> The command could then perhaps allow "latest X" or "exact set of named tags" options?

“Exact set of named tags” can be expressed as a regex. “Latest X” is, in its pure form, impossible, because tags don’t carry timestamps; it would probably need to rely on a specific naming convention.

Hello @mtrmac,

> “Latest X” is, in its pure form, impossible, because tags don’t carry timestamps;

I thought they did? For example a skopeo inspect (or even a docker inspect for that matter) against a particular image tag contains a `Created` property which represents the timestamp. For example, doing a skopeo inspect on `8.3-289` tag of `ubi8` gives:
```
skopeo inspect docker://registry.access.redhat.com/ubi8:8.3-289
```


Is that:

>     "Created": "2021-02-10T00:15:47.908052Z",

not the created date of the tag?

- No, that’s a created date of the _image_; the tag might have been pointed at that image much later
- It’s a property in the image, and requires reading metadata of the image (up to 3 HTTP round-trips)
- It’s optional in the OCI format

>     * No, that’s a created date of the _image_; the tag might have been pointed at that image much later
> 
>     * It’s a property in the image, and requires reading metadata of the image (up to 3 HTTP round-trips)
> 
>     * It’s optional in the OCI format

Thank you for those details. Make sense now. I had forgotten that there's a `docker tag` command which allows you to explicitly create a tag that points to a (tagged) image. Since I always used `docker build ... name:tag`, I thought that the created time of the tag will always be the time that (tagged) image was built.
